item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would they not amnesticize and release them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed. Sorry that I didn't catch that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A minor Nitpick: The Messerschmitt Me 264 strategic bomber was an aborted prototype, it first flew 23. December 1942 and only 3 were ever made.

Thus an "invasion force consists of four battalions and 15 Messerschmitt Me 264's" in the summer of 1941 is impossible. Suggest it is changed to either Junkers Ju 87 Stuka dive bombers or He 111 bombers.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This seems to be a time loop. I'd recommend linking to SCP-176, because they are both time loops that both trap a group of people in a death loop, forever.

In addition, at Levi's request, I'm also proposing a link to an Obskura tale. Most specifically, Aboard the Train to Berlin. This is because this skip involves Obskura.

Editing this:

When a full rotation occurs, all organisms, objects, and events that have occurred or are contained within SCP-2893 return to their original state prior to the reset.

To this:

When a full rotation occurs, all organisms, objects, and events that have occurred or are contained within SCP-2893 return to their original state prior to the reset. This event occurs with a bright flash of light, similar to that of SCP-176.

Editing this:

SCP-2893 was guarded by the paracontainment wing of the French Armed Forces until the Second World War, where it was placed into the control of the Ahnenerbe Obskurakorps1 from 1940 to 1944.

To this:

SCP-2893 was guarded by the paracontainment wing of the French Armed Forces until the Second World War, where it was placed into the control of the Ahnenerbe Obskurakorps2 from 1940 to 1944.

In addition, it's come to my attention that the author of this skip, BananaRepublic, has deleted their Wikidot account. Has somebody taken responsibility for their pages?

Footnotes
1. The paratechnolgical wing of the SS, and the organization responsible for integrating anomalous technology and artifacts into Reich policy. The predecessor to the OBSKURA.
2. The paratechnolgical wing of the SS, and the organization responsible for integrating anomalous technology and artifacts into Reich policy. The predecessor to the OBSKURA.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed - there is no characterisation, and the writing is sloppy and inelegant.

Worse, this seems to disobey its own rules. For example, why does the Foundation feel the need to waste lives defending the barrier when (at that stage) they know that it can't be breached? And "SCP-2893-A instances begin fortifying several locations inside of SCP-2893" is pointless, as those fortifications will reset themselves at the end of the day - the Obskuracorps shouldn't have been impeded by anything in their attack a few days later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing this sentiment. This starts of strong but fails to establish its own identity, with writing weaker than other existing articles with similar concepts. I never really felt anything towards the people trapped in this bubble, I just watched stuff happen to them. The escaping the loop twist at the end felt like it could've elevated this somewhat but the article really fails to deliver anything unique or original in that regard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is almost exactly how I feel. It's still… not quite as visceral or put-together as either of those.

+ show block
– hide block

Focusing on the problems:

I kind of expected something to happen with -11 at the end given how he'd been so built up, so that felt like a wasted opportunity.

The letter seems to be set earlier in the story but comes later in the document, which is weird, especially if the letters were supposed to be appearing in 1949 for a specific reason. Also odd that you say "documents" but only give a single letter. The letter itself didn't really add anything to my perspective on them that I hadn't already gotten (and more effectively/emotionally) from the event logs, so it was kind of a waste of space. Honestly, I'd be much more interested in reading a letter with more info on the time after the alliance under -11, or how that developed, or a letter from -11.

Also really odd to have addendum -A when there are -As, and the footnotes were a bit overused and several could be in that text (especially the one to see the addendum. On mobile, clicking bookmarks takes you to the bottom and then you have to scroll back up).

As TL333s said, the letter could use some proofreading. The containment incidents could too. It's really not clear to me why you repeat their designation in the text that is exclusively about them, when you could say "the instance" or even "him."

No vote. At the very least it needs some cleanup, maybe reordering, and hopefully expansion. There's a lot to like here, but it needs more polish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some part of me wants to denigrate this as 186 meets the basketball DVD. But it's got such a good backstory, it really stands on its own.

That said, the writing seems to break down in quality the further this goes. The letter especially seems poorly edited, and if this was intentional, it doesn't come across as any sort of natural mode of speech. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a crapsack Valhalla. No mead, no feasting, just kill and be killed. Over and over again, until they get out.

And then it gets worse.

I especially liked the Obskurakorps. The blend of zeal and incompetence really represents the Ahnenerbe well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I noticed that as well. I was thinking that maybe the instance had gotten busy real early, but thinking back on it it doesn't make much sense. Changed. Glad you liked it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One nitpick:

SCP-2893-A-876 had attempted to be a part of a funeral service of a Mr. John Taylor. Found to be the Great-Grandson of SCP-2893-A-876.

1952 is only 35 years after the bubble went up. That's a bit fast for great-grandchildren.

Oh and by the way this is overall fantastic. Have a +1.

item.title: Author Page
item.content:encoded:
What happens when you put World War 1 Soldiers in groundhogs day?

I don't know. But have a skip instead.

Thanks to Vacuum, Niles, Avocadonut, lex1nat0r, Lazar, and the people of site19 in general. Couldn't have done it without you.

pictures: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/74/64emeInfanterie.jpg

https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/60/1918_3.MGK_RIR6_Lt.Strauss_Lt.Krummheuer_Off.Stv.Schmitt.JPG
